See Pointes at para. 78, citing Platnick v. Bent, 2018 ONCA 687, aff'd 2020 SCC 23, at para. 99.
[147] On the evidence before me, I do not find that Mr. Cheesman's claim can be characterized as an archetypal SLAPP suit, nor do I find that it was brought in bad faith or for an improper purpose.
[148] Considering the SLAPP indicia referenced above I would not conclude that the evidence shows Mr. Cheesman used litigation, or the threat of it, to silence his critics. There is also no evidence of a financial or power imbalance strongly favouring the plaintiff; rather, the evidence shows that Mr. Cheesman would not have been able to bring this claim without the assistance of the NCCM.
[149] As for the question of whether the purpose of the action is punitive or retributory, I find this characteristic is not present.
[150] The defendants characterize Mr. Cheesman's purpose in bringing this lawsuit as improper and in bad faith, and ask this Court to look at "what is really going on". I do not agree with the defendants' position in this regard. To the extent that I am required to assess "what is really going on" in the litigation, I have reviewed the arguments and evidence relied on by the parties and conclude, on the record before me, there is nothing apparently insidious about this litigation. The fact that I find it should be dismissed under the PPPA does not undermine this finding.
[151] That the action is defective does not mean that it is brought for an improper purpose, which is the test under s. 8. It goes without saying that in most cases one party loses, but a party who loses is not by definition a bad faith actor. The action was commenced by the UBC SJC as co-plaintiff, an allegedly improper party, and I have found the remaining party, Mr. Cheesman, does not have sufficient connection to the expression to sustain the claim. These features may be serious and ultimately incurable flaws, but they do not necessarily depict a suit brought for an improper purpose.
[152] The defendants here are particularly concerned about Mr. Cheesman's evidence that NCCM had a role in his pursuit of the litigation, specifically that the NCCM have agreed to indemnify him for adverse costs awards. The evidence discloses that the NCCM viewed the Sticker as having an Islamophobic message and that Mr. Cheesman and the UBC SJC were affected by Islamophobia due to its publication.
[153] Counsel for Mr. Dobrer relied heavily on an interview Mr. Cheesman gave to a local publication when arguing what the "real purpose" of the litigation was. In that interview, Mr. Cheesman commented that goals of the litigation included to curtail intimidation tactics and remind people of the conflict between Israel and Palestine.
[154] Although he asked about this very subject during cross-examination on his affidavit, counsel for Mr. Dobrer declined to take the court to the sworn testimony of Mr. Cheesman that provided reasons for pursuing litigation which expressly included the aim of clearing his reputation. This omission was not of assistance to the Court's effort to make a decision on the basis of a reasonably accurate assessment of the record.
[155] Mr. Cheesman testified that without the support of the NCCM he would not have been able to pursue the claim, because he did not have the funds to do so. However, he also testified that the rationale for doing so with the financial support that was offered was in order "to clear our names" and "set the record straight that I and the [UBC] SJC do not love Hamas because of the deluge of threats that we received as a result of the stickers. That's why the lawsuit exists." I would not conclude that the fact that an anti-Islamophobic non-profit organization agreed to support an individual and organization who felt they had suffered harm by being falsely portrayed as terrorist-sympathisers diminishes the genuineness or character of the litigation. Whether or not the NCCM supported Mr. Cheesman's litigation does not change the nature of the litigation. The evidence suggests Mr. Cheesman had various reasons for pursuing litigation, some for personal vindication and remedy for reputational harm, others related to his political commitments.
[156] As noted above, the PPPA is intended to address 'strategic' lawsuits against public participation because this type of action has the effect of quashing important public discourse. In Hansman SCC, the Supreme Court of Canada described a SLAPP as not having the goal of a legal victory, "but a political one: to intimidate and suppress criticism with the threat of costly litigation": para. 46. The court further stated that a key feature of a SLAPP is "the strategic use of the legal system to silence contrary viewpoints": Hansman SCC at para. 46. The fact that Mr. Cheesman expresses that his litigation has political facets, does not render the litigation legally problematic in this case. Mr. Cheesman's political motivation expressed in relation to the suit was one of vindication, not silencing--he wanted to clear his name and that of the UBC SJC and ensure that the perspective they advanced was able to remain one that could be heard on campus.
[157] Mr. Cheesman's claim, in my view, cannot be described as one intended to "suppress debate". Without finding what the intention of the Sticker may have been or the value of its expression, on its face it did not appear to be an individual's significant contribution to debate in the meaning understood by the PPPA. The PPPA is a legislative instrument intended to protect the vital right of free expression of individuals whose rights are curtailed by forces larger and more powerful than themselves. Individuals should be able to speak out on matters of public interest. They should be able to do so without unjustified limits imposed on them by the state. While individuals are to be accountable for their false or harmful publications, they should also be able to express themselves without litigation being used coercively to suppress speech rather than protect individual reputation.
[158] Here the speech was not attributed to its author, and was made by a person who did not intend to take credit for it, much less champion the message (although later properly admitted to publishing the Sticker). The speech was made in the guise of expression by the UBC SJC itself and was created in a manner that could reasonably be expected to fool an audience into thinking it was genuine. This does not mean that a defamation claim would succeed in relation to the Sticker. It does, however, weigh in favour of the Plaintiff's argument that his action is not motivated by the desire to suppress opposing viewpoints, but by the proper aims of a defamation suit, being vindication of harms to reputation caused by perceived disinformation.
[159] Although a claim in defamation does not have to be 'archetypal' for the PPPA to apply, as it does in this case, not every claim subject to the PPPA should be viewed in the same light. Having reviewed the evidence relied on, it is my view that the defendants' arguments about the role or participation of the NCCM were misplaced and ultimately unhelpful.
[160] Lastly, while there is an argument to be made that Mr. Cheesman's damages claim is minimal or nominal, it would be premature to make that assessment, and I make no finding as to that SLAPP characteristic here. This factor does not alter my ultimate determination that Mr. Cheesman's claim was not brought in bad faith or for an improper purpose. Therefore, I decline to order damages.
[161] As for the defendants' requests for costs on a full indemnity basis, I do not consider this an appropriate case for full indemnity costs. In Hobbs v. Warner, 2021 BCCA 290, the Court of Appeal considered the circumstances in which such an award might be considered inappropriate, and offered the following guidance:
[104] Section 4 applications will fall along a continuum ranging from lawsuits that have strong indicia of a true SLAPP to those where the competing interests of reputation and public expression are finely balanced. In those cases where there are strong indicia of a SLAPP, a full indemnity award may well be appropriate. The full indemnity starting point is clearly intended to disincentivize such litigation. On the other hand, when the proceeding bears little resemblance to a SLAPP but the public interest in protecting the expression leads to a dismissal of the action, it might well not be fair and reasonable to award full indemnity costs.
[162] I reiterate my findings respecting the SLAPP indicia here. In particular, I repeat that the record suggests that Mr. Cheesman's motivation in bringing this lawsuit was one of vindication, not one aimed at suppressing debate relating to matters of public interest. These factors militate against ordering full indemnity costs against Mr. Cheesman.
[163] Therefore, I conclude that this is not an appropriate case in which full indemnity costs should be ordered. In Simpson v. Rebel News Network Ltd., 2022 BCSC 1160 at para. 99, Justice Milman found that although the plaintiff had failed to meet her burden under s. 4(2)(a) of the PPPA, it did not follow that she had nothing to complain about in how she was treated. I would similarly find that Mr. Cheesman raised a valid concern about the Sticker, and although I have concluded that his suit could not succeed, it was not entirely meritless. The parties will bear their own costs.
VIII. Conclusion
[164] I have found Mr. Cheesman's claim does not have substantial merit. If the Sticker had named him or more closely identified him, I may have concluded differently. Although I would not describe this claim as an archetypal SLAPP, the legislation nevertheless applies. I am allowing the applications under s. 4(2)(a)(i) and Mr. Cheesman's claim is dismissed. I decline to order full indemnity costs or damages. The parties will bear their own costs.
"E. Sigurdson J."
